topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the shared dorm room felt thick, suffocating. I slammed my textbook shut, the sound echoing sharply off the cinderblock walls. My gaze snapped to the corner of my desk, where my personal journal lay, its cover slightly askew. A cold knot tightened in my stomach as I realized it wasn't where I’d left it. I stalked over, my steps heavy, and there, resting open on my notes, was my handwriting, detailing anxieties about midterms and a particularly frustrating conversation with my professor. Beside the entry, a fresh, smudged line of text appeared, clearly written in my looping script, but decidedly not mine. My jaw clenched so tightly my teeth ached. I spun around, my eyes locking onto my roommate, who was lounging on their bed, a placid, almost bored expression on their face. "What in the actual hell are you doing?" My voice came out low and dangerously steady, every syllable laced with barely contained fury. They just blinked slowly, a faint, unreadable smirk playing on their lips.

[story 2]

I found the evidence tucked beneath my pillow. My heart started hammering against my ribs, a frantic drum solo against the silence of the room. It was my journal, pages splayed open, and someone had been reading it. The sheer audacity of it made my hands shake. I marched over to the desk, my knuckles white as I gripped the edge. The ink on the page was disturbed, a passage I’d written about a secret fear, now annotated with strange, looping script. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of pure indignation washing over me. "Stop it," I hissed, the word sharp and brittle. I didn't wait for a response. I snatched the book, my movements jerky and aggressive, and threw it onto the floor, the sound startlingly loud. I stared at my roommate, who was now sitting up, their eyes wide with a sudden, defensive flicker. The silence stretched, heavy and charged, punctuated only by the frantic thumping of my own pulse.

[story 3]

A slow, building pressure began to mount behind my eyes. I hadn't noticed anything amiss until I picked up my notebook; it felt subtly wrong. The pages were turned past the last entry, and a section I’d sworn was private was now filled with observations, written in a handwriting that was undeniably familiar, yet alien. A slow, simmering resentment began to boil in my chest, quickly transforming into something hot and volatile. I stood up, my chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor—a deliberate, jarring noise. "Don't," I managed, the sound raspy, stripped bare of any pleasantry. My gaze was fixed on my roommate, who looked up from their laptop, an expression of mild surprise crossing their features. I took a deliberate step toward them, my posture rigid, radiating a palpable sense of being profoundly offended. I couldn't quite form the words, only a tight, strained silence that spoke volumes about my displeasure.
